data.title: China's Q1 GDP expands 1.20% Q/Q
data.description:
China's GDP expanded 1.20% in the first quarter of 2025 over the previous quarter. 

The Gross Domestic Product in China expanded 5.40 percent in the first quarter of 2025 over the same quarter of the previous year.

Separate data showed, China’s new home prices in 70 cities fell by 4.6% year-on-year in March 2025, easing slightly from a 4.8% decline in February.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSE:TDF), (NYSEARCA:CHIQ), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:KBA), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:CWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:KURE).

Currency: (CNY:USD)

More on China markets and economy

Trump intensifies tariff pressure: "No one off the hook," denies tariff rollbacks

Trump says his administration will look at 'whole electronics supply chain'

Trump issues 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs except for China

data.title: Omnicom projects completion of IPG acquisition by H2 2025 with $750M cost synergy target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Omnicom Group Inc. (OMC) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Wren highlighted Q1 2025 organic revenue growth of 3.4%, supported by strong performance in media, advertising, and precision marketing disciplines. Adjusted EBITDA margin stood at 13.8%, and non-GAAP adjusted EPS was $1.70, an increase of 1.8% year-over-year.
  • Wren discussed progress on the proposed acquisition of Interpublic, with stockholder votes completed in March and five regulatory approvals secured out of 18 required jurisdictions. The company targets $750 million in run-rate cost synergies post-acquisition.
  • On AI initiatives, Wren emphasized advancements driven by Omni AI, which is being integrated across agency workflows to enhance creativity, targeting, and operational efficiency. Plans are in place to expand Omni AI deployment to all client-facing employees by year-end.
  • CFO Phil Angelastro noted that foreign currency translation impacted Q1 revenues by (1.6%) and discussed the company’s continued focus on efficiency initiatives to manage costs.

Outlook

  • The company revised its full-year 2025 organic growth guidance to a range of 2.5% to 4.5%, citing macroeconomic uncertainty and potential client conservatism. Adjusted EBITDA margin guidance remains at 10 basis points above the 2024 level of 15.5%.
  • Management indicated confidence in achieving $750 million in cost synergies following the Interpublic acquisition, with integration planning actively underway.

Financial Results

  • Q1 2025 organic revenue growth was 3.4%, with media and advertising up 7%, precision marketing up 6%, and public relations down 5%. The healthcare sector declined 3%, while branding and retail commerce fell 10% due to market uncertainty and reduced M&A activity.
  • Total reported operating expenses included $33.8 million in IPG acquisition-related costs. Adjusted EBITDA grew 1.6% year-over-year, maintaining a margin of 13.8%.
  • Free cash flow for the 12 months ended March 31, 2025, grew by 3.5%, supported by improved operating income. Share repurchases, which resumed after the March stockholder vote, are expected to reach $600 million for the full year.

Q&A

  • Adam Berlin, UBS: Asked about the rationale behind lowering the organic growth guidance range. CEO Wren explained that the adjustment reflects caution amid economic uncertainty, particularly in events and other discretionary spending areas.
  • David Karnovsky, JPMorgan: Inquired about delays in public relations and branding sectors. CFO Angelastro attributed the PR decline to reduced U.S. government spending, while the branding slowdown was linked to weaker M&A activity and market conditions.
  • Jason Bazinet, Citigroup: Asked about when revenue synergies from the IPG acquisition might materialize. Wren stated that no significant client disruptions are expected and reiterated confidence in achieving cost synergies and revenue growth post-acquisition.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about economic uncertainty, specifically its impact on organic growth and discretionary client spending. The tone was slightly cautious but constructive.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking stance, emphasizing operational resilience, strategic progress on the IPG acquisition, and AI-driven efficiencies. Wren stated, "We remain on track to close the acquisition of Interpublic in the second half of the year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone reflected increased caution due to macroeconomic volatility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Organic revenue growth slowed from Q4 2024’s 5.2% to 3.4% in Q1 2025 due to weaker performance in healthcare, public relations, and branding disciplines.
  • Guidance language shifted to a more cautious tone, expanding the 2025 organic growth range downward to 2.5%.
  • Analysts’ questions focused more on macroeconomic challenges this quarter, whereas prior discussions were more focused on growth opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited economic volatility and potential client conservatism as key risks to 2025 performance.
  • Analysts raised concerns about specific sectors, including branding, public relations, and healthcare, which are underperforming or facing delays.
  • Mitigation strategies include continued cost management, leveraging AI tools, and maintaining strong client relationships to navigate uncertainty.

Final Takeaway

Omnicom began 2025 with steady organic revenue growth of 3.4% and made significant progress toward completing its acquisition of Interpublic, targeting $750 million in cost synergies. While management maintained confidence in its strategic initiatives, it adopted a cautious stance due to macroeconomic uncertainty, revising full-year organic growth guidance to a wider range of 2.5% to 4.5%. The company continues to invest in AI-driven efficiencies and integration planning, signaling long-term growth potential.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump targets Medicare price negotiations in new executive order
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Tuesday signed an executive order to lower prescription drug prices through a series of measures, including a requirement to change a key provision in the Medicare drug price negotiation program.

The order directed Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to work with lawmakers to address a long-standing issue called the “pill penalty” in the negotiation process, which the industry group had said discouraged pharmaceutical companies from investing in small molecule drugs.

Under former President Joe Biden’s Inflation Reduction Act in 2022, pills, tablets, and other small-molecule drugs faced Medicare price setting four years earlier than large molecules such as biologics following FDA approval.

“This imbalance exacerbates incentives that already too often push companies to invest in large molecule drugs over small molecule alternatives, which often apply to broader populations and impact different types of conditions,” a White House official said on Tuesday.

Additionally, Trump’s executive order envisions an improvement to the Medicare price negotiation program’s 22% savings it achieved in the first year, and attempts to standardize Medicare payments for prescription drugs, such as cancer treatments.

The order also requires HHS to align Medicare payments for certain prescription drugs to the cost paid by hospitals, a measure that will lower the government’s current payments by 35%, according to the White House.

“The Biden-Harris Administration let many of these priorities languish while failing to even achieve the savings projected from the new Medicare Prescription Drug Negotiation Program,” the White House said in a statement.

In 2023, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) which oversees Medicare, announced the first 10 Part D drugs selected for pricing negotiations, which will lead to revised prices in 2026.

Blockbuster meds from Bristol Myers (NYSE:BMY), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), AbbVie (ABBV), Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ), AstraZeneca (AZN), Novartis' (NVS), and Merck (MRK). Amgen (AMGN) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) were part of the initial list.

The CMS has already selected 15 additional Medicare Part D drugs for the second round of pricing negotiations.

Popular medications from the likes of Novo Nordisk (NVO), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), Bristol Myers (NYSE:BMY), AbbVie (ABBV)/Ironwood Pharma (IRWD), AstraZeneca (AZN), Teva (TEVA), Theravance Biopharma (TBPH), Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), Bausch Health (BHC), AbbVie (ABBV), Merck (MRK), and Amgen (AMGN) were part of that list announced in January.

data.title: Rio Tinto posts lowest Q1 iron ore shipments since 2019, sees 2025 at low end of guidance
data.description:
Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) said Tuesday Q1 iron ore shipments were its weakest since 2019 for a first quarter result and warned that more weather disruptions could lead to a 2025 forecast miss.

In a quarterly update, Rio (NYSE:RIO) reported Q1 shipments of 70.7M metric tons from Australia's Pilbara region, 9% lower than the year-earlier uqarter and 17% below shipments in Q4 2024, while also missing a Visible Alpha consensus estimate of 73.6M tons.

Rio (RIO) previously estimated it lost ~13M tons in shipments due to a series of tropical cyclones during the quarter that impacted activities at the Dampier port in the Pilbara region.

"Mitigation plans are in place to offset around half of this and will require an additional investment of ~A$150M (US$95M) for rectification works and contracting mining activities," the company said, adding that lower volumes and recovery costs will be offset by a weaker than expected Australian dollar.

Rio (RIO) said it now expects Pilbara iron ore shipments for 2025 to hit the lower end of its guidance range of 323M-338M tons, and "the system has limited ability to mitigate further losses from weather if incurred."

Q1 copper production on a consolidated basis rose 16% Y/Y to 210K tons but fell 8% Q/Q; copper output at the Kennecott operation in Utah increased 7% Y/Y but plunged 32% Q/Q due to unplanned conveyor failures; the affected conveyor has since been restored to full functionality, the company said.

data.title: Hancock Whitney outlines 2025 guidance with fee income growth of 9-10% and loan expansion targets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hancock Whitney Corporation (HWC) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Hairston highlighted the company's strong performance, including a 1.41% return on assets (ROA), fee income growth, and net interest margin (NIM) expansion to 3.43%. He also noted the updated guidance reflecting the Sabal Trust acquisition, with fee income expected to rise 9-10% year-over-year and loan growth projected in low single digits for 2025, predominantly in the second half of the year.

  • The Sabal Trust transaction, set to close on May 2, positions Hancock Whitney to expand its regional banking services in Florida, specifically in Tampa and Orlando.

  • CFO Mike Achary reported net income of $120 million or $1.38 per share for the quarter. He mentioned ongoing improvements in cost management, with expenses increasing only 1% from the prior quarter, and NIM expansion driven by controlled funding costs and favorable funding mix. The company expects NII growth of 3-4% for 2025.

  • Achary emphasized that capital returns remain a focus, with 350,000 shares repurchased and a 50% year-over-year increase in the common stock dividend to $0.45 per share.

Outlook

  • The company updated its guidance for 2025, projecting low single-digit loan growth, with most growth occurring in the second half of the year. The Sabal Trust acquisition is expected to contribute to a 9-10% increase in fee income.

  • NIM is anticipated to continue modest expansion, supported by repricing of CDs and cash flows from bond and fixed-rate loan portfolios. The guidance includes assumptions of three 25-basis-point rate cuts in 2025.

  • Non-interest expenses are forecasted to increase 4-5% year-over-year, including costs associated with the Sabal Trust integration.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q1 2025 was $120 million, a slight decrease from $122 million in Q4 2024. Pre-provision net revenue (PPNR) was $162.4 million, down marginally from the prior quarter but up 6% year-over-year.

  • Deposits declined by $298 million, primarily due to seasonal public fund outflows. Retail CDs decreased following reductions in promotional rates, aiding in controlling deposit costs. Non-interest-bearing deposit (DDA) balances increased $18 million, maintaining a 36% mix.

  • Loan balances fell by $201 million, driven by higher payoffs in large healthcare and commercial non-real estate loans, offsetting strong production in other areas.

  • The company reported a cost of funds decrease to 1.59%, with CDs repricing at lower rates. Bond portfolio yields rose to 2.78%, supported by reinvestment at higher yields.

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about potential for increased buybacks given capital accretion and slower loan growth. CFO Achary responded that buybacks are expected at current or slightly higher levels, contingent on external conditions.

  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Inquired about hiring plans and their impact on growth. CEO Hairston stated that the company aims to hire 20-30 new bankers in 2025, with growth expected to accelerate in the back half of the year.

  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler: Questioned the drivers of enhanced PPNR guidance. CFO Achary noted that fee income growth, including contributions from the Sabal Trust acquisition, and controlled expenses are key factors.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on loan growth challenges and credit quality. Questions indicated a desire for clarity on growth drivers and capital deployment strategies.

  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing strategic initiatives such as the Sabal Trust acquisition, expense management, and deposit cost controls. CEO Hairston underscored the company's readiness to navigate market volatility and capitalize on growth opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to highlight the impact of the Sabal Trust acquisition, with fee income growth projections increasing from 3.5-4.5% in Q4 2024 to 9-10% in Q1 2025.

  • Loan growth guidance was reduced from mid-single-digit to low single-digit, reflecting client sentiment and macroeconomic uncertainties.

  • Analysts' focus shifted from general performance metrics to specific growth strategies and capital deployment.

  • Management's tone remained steady, with increased emphasis on leveraging strategic acquisitions and controlling costs.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential headwinds from macroeconomic trends, particularly client sentiment and rate environment impacts on loan growth.

  • Analysts raised concerns about credit quality, noting increases in criticized loans and the potential impacts of prolonged macroeconomic uncertainty.

  • Management highlighted robust liquidity, strong capital ratios, and a solid allowance for credit losses as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Hancock Whitney Corporation demonstrated strong financial performance in Q1 2025, driven by disciplined cost management, strategic acquisitions, and focused capital deployment. The Sabal Trust acquisition is expected to enhance fee income growth and expand the company’s footprint in key Florida markets. While loan growth guidance has been moderated, the company’s confidence in its hiring strategy and market positioning suggests growth opportunities in the latter half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil edges lower as gloomy oil demand forecasts pile up
data.description:
Crude oil futures tilted slightly lower Tuesday as markets steadied after two weeks of tariff-driven volatility.

Future demand remained at the top of market concerns, with the International Energy Agency cutting its 2025 demand growth estimate by ~300K bbl/day, anticipating a negative impact of U.S. tariffs on global economic growth, following demand downgrades by OPEC and the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

World oil demand this year will rise by 730K bbl/day, the IEA said in its monthly report, a sharp cut from slightly more than 1M bbl/day forecast last month and the weakest demand growth since 2020, when demand contracted because of the COVID-19 pandemic.

"The deteriorating outlook for the global economy amid the sudden sharp escalation in trade tensions in early April has prompted a downgrade to our forecast for oil demand growth this year," the IEA wrote, with roughly half the decline coming from the U.S. and China, and most of the remainder from trade-oriented Asian economies.

In its first look at 2026, the IEA forecast a further slowdown in demand growth to 690K bbl/day due to a fragile economic backdrop and growing penetration of electric vehicles.

The IEA sharply cut its demand estimates, while OPEC remains more optimistic, but both acknowledged U.S. tariffs have hurt consumption.

"The uncertainty is generating significant headwinds for global growth and, as a result, oil demand," ING analysts wrote, adding that traders will now closely watch the next phase in global trade policies as well as U.S.-Iran talks set to be held on Saturday.

Several banks, including UBS, BNP Paribas and HSBC, have cut their crude oil price forecasts in recent days.

Meanwhile, the Keystone pipeline that carries Canadian oil to the U.S. Midwest restarted after completing repairs following last week's spill.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery closed -0.3% to $61.33/bbl, and front-month June Brent crude (CO1:COM) also finished -0.3% to $64.67/bbl, the fourth lowest settlement value YTD for both benchmarks; front-month May Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) ended +0.1% to $3.329/MMBtu.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

The EIA projected U.S. oil production will rise from 13.7M bbl/day this year before peaking at 14M bbl/day in 2027 and maintain that level through the end of the decade, before dropping to 11.3M bbl/day in 2050.

U.S. shale oil production will peak at 10M bbl/day in 2027, up from 9.69M bbl/day anticipated this year, before declining to 9.33M bbl/day by 2050, the agency said.

The U.S. Department of Energy blamed former President Biden's policies for charting a "disastrous path" for American energy production and said the EIA outlook is based on policies in place at the end of last year.

data.title: Interactive brokers signals 32% account growth and raises dividend amid strong Q1 results
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Interactive Brokers (IBKR) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Milan Galik highlighted a 32% growth in total accounts, driven by the addition of 279,000 new accounts in the quarter, with international growth leading. He emphasized the popularity of options and futures trading, noting a 25% and 16% increase in contract volumes respectively, both achieving quarterly records.
  • Management emphasized the resilience of their model, noting that "Interactive Brokers does not need upmarkets to generate revenue" as customers remained active despite market volatility.
  • CFO Paul Brody stated that commission revenue reached $0.5 billion for the first time, driven by higher trading volumes and increased client activity. Adjusted pre-tax profit margin stood at 74%, reflecting strong expense management.
  • The company announced a dividend increase to $0.32 per quarter and a four-for-one stock split to enhance liquidity and accessibility.

Outlook

  • Management reported continued strong client inflows, with significant interest from international markets. Clients showed an appetite for U.S. investments despite geopolitical concerns.
  • Milan Galik described ongoing growth in client equity, which rose 23% year-over-year to $573.5 billion, and client credit balances, which increased 19% to $125.2 billion.
  • The company plans to expand its cryptocurrency offerings and introduce additional products internationally, including Canadian first home savings accounts and NIFTY 50 index futures in Singapore.

Financial Results

  • Total net revenues reached a record high, supported by a 36% year-over-year increase in commissions and a 3% rise in net interest income to $770 million.
  • Expenses were well-controlled, with compensation and benefits ratio at 11% of adjusted net revenues. Execution clearing and distribution costs were $121 million.
  • Other fees and services revenue increased 32% from the previous year to $78 million, driven by higher-risk exposure fees and payments for order flow.
  • The company maintained its focus on short-term yields in its investment portfolio, with segregated cash interest income impacted by central bank rate changes.

Q&A

  • James Yarrow, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the impact of retail pressure and client allocation shifts. CEO Galik noted significant trading volumes and diversification into fixed-income instruments and foreign exchange. He reported a 10%-12% decrease in margin loans due to market volatility.
  • Craig Siegenthaler, Bank of America: Questioned the potential impact of trade conflicts on non-U.S. appetite for U.S. markets. Galik confirmed strong international account growth and emphasized the platform's ability to offer both local and U.S. market access.
  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: Inquired about the expansion of crypto offerings. Galik cited regulatory changes and increased client demand as drivers for adding new cryptocurrencies and increasing account exposure limits to crypto assets.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about market volatility and its effects on client activity and margin loans. The tone was generally skeptical but acknowledged the company’s ability to adapt.
  • Management remained confident, highlighting platform strengths and resilience. Galik frequently pointed to client inflows and trading activity as indicators of robust performance despite market challenges.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was slightly more defensive amidst discussions of market risks, though confidence in long-term strategies remained evident.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Account additions in Q1 2025 exceeded those in Q4 2024, with 279,000 new accounts compared to 217,000 previously.
  • Dividend increased to $0.32 per quarter from $0.25, reflecting enhanced capital allocation.
  • Growth in options and futures trading volumes outpaced prior quarter levels, highlighting client engagement in diverse asset classes.
  • Management maintained a strong focus on international expansion and new product introductions, consistent with previous strategic priorities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted challenges in finding suitable acquisition targets due to valuation mismatches.
  • Analysts questioned the impact of geopolitical tensions and tariff news on international client engagement with U.S. markets. Management expressed confidence in the platform's global appeal.
  • Margin loan balances saw a 12% decline early in Q2 due to market volatility, with management describing this as within expectations.

Final Takeaway

Interactive Brokers delivered robust Q1 2025 results, achieving record revenues and strong client growth metrics. The company's global reach and automated platform facilitated resilience amidst market volatility. Strategic initiatives, including expanded product offerings and a dividend increase, underscore confidence in sustainable growth. Management's focus on innovation and international expansion positions the company to navigate evolving market dynamics effectively.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Element Solutions upgraded at Bank of America on resilient earnings
data.description:
Element Solutions (NYSE:ESI) on Tuesday was upgraded to Buy from a previous investment rating of Neutral by analysts at Bank of America. They pointed to the specialty-chemical company’s resilient earnings profile and exposure to high-growth markets, despite recent share price declines and broader macroeconomic challenges.

Analyst Steve Byrne highlighted that ESI’s (NYSE:ESI) global manufacturing footprint — 55 localized production sites situated near customers — helps mitigate the effect of new trade tariffs. This geographic flexibility helps the company shift operations efficiently in response to trade disruptions, shielding it from many of the direct cost pressures facing other materials and specialty chemical producers.

Some indirect exposure remains, especially for customers in the printed circuit board supply chain that export products, and Byrne said that recent U.S. tariff exemptions on smartphones and computers reduce near-term demand risk.

These key consumer electronics products will mostly be subject to the 20% tariff targeting Chinese goods related to fentanyl production, but are likely to experience only minimal additional tariffs elsewhere. As a result, pricing stability in the electronics market appears more sustainable than initially feared.

Byrne also pointed to ESI’s (ESI) ability to pass through cost inflation due to the specialized, nondiscretionary nature of its products. Even in a broader inflationary environment, ESI’s low-cost and essential inputs make it well-positioned to preserve margins, he said.

Possible entry point

Despite this operational stability, ESI (ESI) shares have dropped 34% in the past two months — an overreaction that now presents a compelling entry point, according to Bank of America.

ESI’s (ESI) exposure to structurally expanding sectors such as electric vehicles, data storage and AI computing also sets it apart in the chemicals space, offering relative insulation from a potential recession. The company’s recent divestiture of its Graphics Solutions business in the first quarter further concentrates its portfolio in electronics, enhancing its growth profile and justifying a higher long-term valuation multiple.

Bank of America lowered its price target for ESI (ESI) to $24 from $29 a share, citing broader sector-wide multiple compression. The new target is based on an estimated enterprise value that’s 13 times earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization for 2025 earnings, down from a previous estimate of 14.6 times.

The analysts kept their first-quarter 2025 ebitda forecast unchanged at $125 million, but trimmed their full-year estimate to $521 million from $545 million. The revision reflects expectations for slightly softer second-half demand, particularly low single-digit declines in its Industrial & Specialty segment, and some cost pressure from tariffs. These challenges are expected to be partially offset by favorable foreign exchange rates and improving conditions in China’s electronics and automotive sectors.

data.title: Citi still bearish on copper but sees prices falling more slowly as U.S. tariffs ease
data.description:
Citi analysts are forecasting a slower drop in copper prices over the next three months, noting that President Trump relaxed some tariffs, China has bought on dips, and scrap supply remains tight due to U.S. stockpiling, Reuters reported Tuesday.

All signs "point to a more gradual copper price decline through 2Q 2025 vs. the deeper and faster investor selloff we previously anticipated, with funds still positioned net bullish," Citi wrote, raising its three-month copper forecast to $8,800/oz, a week after reducing its price outlook to $8,000/oz following Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcements.

However, Citi said it remains bearish over the next 3-6 as physical copper consumption and manufacturing activity slow down due to tariffs, especially the 145% levy on China.

Analysts at J.P. Morgan are bearish on industrial metals in the near term, seeing copper averaging just $8,300/ton in Q2, believing cuts to economic expectations due to tariff uncertainty and the increased probability of a recession in 2025 mean steep cuts to metal demand estimates.

JPM trimmed its global copper demand forecast by 1%, with risks to demand estimates still skewed to the downside, as focus increasingly shifts to China's possible reactions as export demand is expected to fall, including potential fiscal stimulus.

Front-month Comex copper (HG1:COM) for April delivery finished flat Tuesday at $4.611/lb, following a five-day winning streak in which the metal jumped 11.7%.

ETFs: (CPER), (COPX), (OTC:JJCTF)

Major players in the U.S. copper industry including Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) have called on President Trump to restrict exports of ore and scrap metal rather than impose tariffs on imports, Bloomberg reported.

"The Trump administration should consider implementing export restrictions on domestically produced copper concentrate and copper scrap," Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) wrote, and Southwire - the largest U.S. manufacturer of copper wire, said "the administration should focus on regulatory reform and restrictions on U.S. copper exports as the primary tools to grow the U.S. industry."

Freeport McMoRan (FCX) did not make direct recommendation about tariffs, but argued the U.S. should support free trade, saying "promoting free and fair trade with U.S. allies will ensure U.S. copper supply requirements are satisfied," and noting the U.S. imported roughly half of its copper cathode demand from Chile, Canada, Peru and other countries in 2024, "which is necessary to meet current demand because there is no U.S. latent production capacity."

data.title: U.S. government informs Nvidia it requires license to export H20 hardware to China
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) received a notice from the U.S. government that it needs a special license to ship its H20 graphic processing units or related hardware to China, and such a license will be required indefinitely.

Due to this development, Nvidia expects to take a charge of $5.5B in its upcoming quarterly financial results, according to a Form 8-K it filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

Nvidia shares dropped 6% during early post-market trading on Tuesday.

"On April 9, 2025, the U.S. government, or USG, informed NVIDIA Corporation, or the Company, that the USG requires a license for export to China (including Hong Kong and Macau) and D:5 countries, or to companies headquartered or with an ultimate parent therein, of the Company's H20 integrated circuits and any other circuits achieving the H20's memory bandwidth, interconnect bandwidth, or combination thereof," the report said. "The USG indicated that the license requirement addresses the risk that the covered products may be used in, or diverted to, a supercomputer in China. On April 14, 2025, the USG informed the Company that the license requirement will be in effect for the indefinite future."

"First quarter results are expected to include up to approximately $5.5 billion of charges associated with H20 products for inventory, purchase commitments, and related reserves," Nvidia said in the filing.

Nvidia declined to comment on the issue.

Although Nvidia's more advanced artificial intelligence GPUs have already been banned for export to China, the H20 chip was designed specifically to comply with export restrictions.

The H20 is the most advanced chip offered by Nvidia that was still allowed for export to China. However, industry observers and Chinese tech companies had expected the H20 to be added to the list of other banned hardware. Last week, a report surfaced that the Trump Administration was pausing the implementation of an export ban to China for Nvidia's H20 GPUs. 

Alibaba (BABA), Tencent (TCEHY)(TCTZF) and ByteDance (BDNCE) placed at least $16B in orders for Nvidia's H20 server chips in the first three months of 2025, ahead of a potential U.S. ban on the sale of the AI chip in China.

Early on Tuesday morning, U.S. President Donald Trump posted on Truth Social that Nvidia had committed $500B to build AU supercomputers in the U.S.

"This is very big and exciting news," he said. "All necessary permits will be expedited and quickly delivered to NVIDIA, as they will to all companies committing to be part of the Golden Age of America!"
